unitTitle: Y7 Programming essentials in Scratch — part I (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Sequencing — Frère Jacques
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that computers need precise instructions
I can say what a sequence is
I can predict what a sequence of blocks will do
I can put music blocks in the right order to play a song
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: sequence · instruction · execute · subroutine. TA: everyone has Scratch open; headphones for the music task. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — robots are not like people (think–pair–share): computers need precise instructions. Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet (no writing).
   • Support: tick the word ‘sequence’.  • Core: name one thing a computer needs that a person can guess.  • Challenge: why was “turn right” not enough for the robot?
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do. Teacher plays the short commands video (teacher-played hook, has sound), then defines a sequence with the music-score / recipe / furniture image. Class predicts what the Frère Jacques program will play (PRIMM Predict).
   • Support: first line placed for them.  • Core: predict in words.  • Challenge: explain why subroutines help. Likely error: blocks in the wrong order → fix-words “click one block to hear it, then check it comes next.”
3. (20–45) You-do — order the eight song lines (each sung twice) on the worksheet, then build the sequence in Scratch (pair programming, swap each task). Keep a screenshot of the working program.
4. (45–55) Plenary — “sequence the sounds” run live if time; pupils self-tick the four “I can…” and play their song. TA notes who got a working sequence.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing; the video/audio are the teacher’s low-arousal choice, played once.

lessons.title: Variables — Chat with Big Ed
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what a variable is (a name for stored data)
I can follow input → process → output
I can predict what a program that uses a variable will do
I can trace the value of a variable as a program runs
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: variable · store · input · process · output · trace. TA: everyone has Scratch open. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–15) Starter — variables storytime: pupils hold values for variable names (TOWN changes half-way). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet. Key point (misconception M1): a variable holds ONE value at a time.
   • Support: tick ‘stored data’.  • Core: what did HERO stand for?  • Challenge: why mattered when TOWN changed?
2. (15–18) Input → process → output: keyboard in, screen out, variable stores in between.
3. (18–48) Chat with Big Ed (PRIMM). Predict the ‘Hello [name]’ code (I do → we do), then pairs investigate the variable blocks and modify (add the music question). Keep a screenshot.
   • Support: identify which orange block sets the variable.  • Core: explain what `name` stores + why ask before say.  • Challenge: what happens to the stored name when a second person types. Likely error: thinks ‘Hello’ shows without the name → “the variable fills only after it is set.”
4. (48–55) Plenary — trace the `temperature` variable (write each value Big Ed says); self-tick the four “I can…”. TA notes who traced correctly. Homework: 4 multiple-choice questions.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement break as routine; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: Chat with Big Ed — variable blocks.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

resources.title: Variables — Chat with Big Ed activity worksheet.md

lessons.title: Selection — If statements
lessons.objectives:
I can say whether a condition is true or false
I can explain what selection (an If statement) does
I can spot where an If statement is needed
I can change a program to use selection
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: condition · expression · evaluate · selection · If statement. TA: everyone has Scratch open. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — true or false? A condition is checked and is either true or false (optional true-side/false-side of the room). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: a condition is only true or false.  • Core: write a true condition.  • Challenge: the ‘this statement is false’ paradox.
2. (7–18) If… else… (I do → we do). The birthday If/else image: true → If part runs, false → else runs. Misconception M21/M22: the else is NOT optional and NOT always run — exactly one path runs.
3. (18–48) Chat with Big Ed (selection PRIMM). Predict, investigate, then modify: add the ‘Ed’ Easter egg (single If) and the ‘not OK’ reply (If → If/else). Keep a screenshot.
   • Support: the Easter egg needs only one If.  • Core: difference between If and If/else; why both are written.  • Challenge: use Or so ‘Ed’ or ‘Big Ed’ both work. Likely error: thinks both messages show → “only one path runs each time.”
4. (48–55) Plenary — Parson’s problem: order the capital-of-Spain quiz code (ask → if correct add score → else incorrect). Self-tick the four “I can…”. TA notes who used selection correctly.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: Selection — If/else blocks.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Operators — comparison & logic
lessons.objectives:
I can use comparison operators (>, <, =) in a condition
I can use logic operators (and, or, not) in a condition
I can decide whether a condition is true or false
I can add a quiz question using operators
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: operator · comparison · logic · condition · evaluate. TA: everyone has Scratch open; jumbo playing cards for the unplugged task. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what will the program output? `score > 30` with score 42. Pupils CHOOSE true/false on the starter worksheet (links to maths’ >, <).
   • Support: `>` means greater than.  • Core: what `<` means.  • Challenge: is 30 > 30?
2. (5–18) Comparison (>, <, =) then logic (and, or, not) operators, using the ‘value = King and suit = hearts’ image. Optional playing-card stand-up: read a Boolean expression, pupils move to true/false; build complexity slowly.
3. (18–43) Worked example — The Big Quiz (I do): add a question accepting more than one answer using `or` (deliberate `and` mistake first, let them catch it). Then you-do: build q2 and work the worksheet.
   • Support: which operator means ‘both’.  • Core: when is `and` false / what `not` does.  • Challenge: the year-group question uses `>` to choose which questions to ask. Likely error: uses `and` where ‘either answer’ is allowed → “‘or’ is true when at least one side is true.”
4. (43–55) Plenary — True or false table (peer-mark if time); self-tick the four “I can…”. TA notes who built a working operator condition.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: Operators — comparison and logic blocks.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Count-controlled iteration & debugging
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what iteration (a loop) is
I can say why loops are useful
I can use a count-controlled loop to make a program shorter
I can find and fix a bug by tracing a variable
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iteration · count-controlled · repeat · debug · trace. TA: everyone has Scratch open. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — spot the pattern: ten `say` blocks counting 1–10; what stays the same / changes? Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: repeating a step is ‘repetition’.  • Core: how many say blocks were used.  • Challenge: what could make it shorter.
2. (5–11) What is iteration? Count-controlled = repeat a set number of times (real example: writing lines). 3. (11–26) Worked example (I do → we do): live-code Counting Cat — replace ten blocks with `repeat 10` + a `number` variable + `change number by 1`. Deliberate mistakes (Forever block; set-value inside the loop) to catch. Start value goes BEFORE the loop.
4. (26–41) You-do — order the loop on the worksheet (Parson’s), then build Ten Green Bottles (pair programming, swap every 5 min). Keep a screenshot.
   • Support: blocks inside the repeat are the repeated ones.  • Core: what iteration means / why a loop beats ten blocks.  • Challenge: count DOWN from 10. Likely error: `change number by 1` omitted → the number never moves.
5. (41–55) Plenary — debugging: the times-table program (every line shows the same answer). Add ‘say the variables’, trace the table, find the bug (`multiply-by` never increments). Introduce bug/debug (Grace Hopper’s moth). Self-tick the four “I can…”. Homework: 4 multiple-choice questions.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: Spot the pattern — ten say blocks.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

resources.title: Count-controlled loop — times table blocks.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Problem-solving — Moves like Jim
lessons.objectives:
I can remember the key programming words from this unit
I can pick the right block to solve a problem
I can combine sequence, selection, loops and operators in one program
I can rate my own confidence honestly
lessons.outline:
Routine first (final lesson of the unit — work as independently as you can). Vocabulary on board: sequence · variable · selection · iteration · operator. TA: everyone has Scratch open. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — beat the teacher: write down as many unit key words as you can in 60 seconds, swap and tick. Then pupils TICK the key words on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: name the count-controlled loop.  • Core: what a variable is used for.  • Challenge: explain a key word in your own words.
2. (5–40) Moves like Jim — the summative dance game. Tasks get harder with less code given. Be-a-problem-solver routine: re-read → check earlier work → ask a partner to prompt → then ask the teacher. Keep a screenshot of each subroutine.
   • Support: copy the shape of the subroutine above; a calm ‘plan it on paper’ role is fine.  • Core: shorten the countdown with a loop; pick the operator for ‘1 or 9’.  • Challenge: score + feedback explorer tasks (`score > 2`). Likely error: uses `and` where ‘either key’ is meant → “‘or’ is true when at least one side is true.”
3. (40–55) Plenary — You’re hired: pupils tick which skills they are confident using and name one to practise next; self-tick the four “I can…”. TA/teacher uses the self-rating to plan support for the next unit (which starts with a Dance Battle).
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound.
